title: The Atlantic became a highway
content: Before the 1500s, the Atlantic Ocean separated continents. European ships changed that by opening new trade routes — and these routes would soon connect to one of history's largest forced migrations.

title: Three continents, one system
content: Europe supplied ships and manufactured goods. West Africa was targeted for captive labor. The Americas held vast land but few workers. Together they formed a single loop of exchange.

title: The first crossings
content: The earliest transatlantic voyages carrying enslaved Africans set the pattern. What began as a trickle would grow into a system spanning centuries.

title: Sugar changed everything
content: Plantation crops like sugar were enormously profitable — and enormously labor-intensive. The hunger for forced labor on these plantations drove the trade's rapid expansion.

title: The Middle Passage
content: The forced Atlantic crossing was the system's cruelest leg. Packed below decks for weeks, many did not survive the voyage from Africa to the Americas.

title: A trade at its peak
content: By the 1700s the trade reached its height, moving more people across the Atlantic than in any prior century and reshaping societies on every shore it touched.

title: The beginning of the end
content: Growing abolition movements and new laws began to ban the trade in the early 1800s. Ending it took decades — and its consequences are still felt today.
